past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 10 years ago, managed with medication
Benign prostatic hyperplasia, diagnosed 3 years ago
Chronic constipation, worsening over the past 3 years
Recurrent urinary tract infections over the past 18 months
Orthostatic dizziness, intermittent for the past year
family_history: Father died at age 78 of myocardial infarction, no neurological disease
Mother alive at age 84, has essential tremor
No known family history of Parkinson's disease, dementia, or atypical parkinsonism

social_history.occupation: Civil engineer, currently working full-time but reports difficulty keeping up with project deadlines
social_history.living_situation: Lives with his wife in a single-story home. Two adult children nearby
social_history.tobacco: Former smoker, quit 20 years ago, 10 pack-year history
social_history.exercise: Previously active, played recreational tennis weekly; stopped 8 months ago due to balance concerns and feeling 'clumsy'

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, date, and situation. MMSE 28/30, losing points on serial sevens (1 error) and recall (missed 1 of 3 words even with category cue). MoCA 25/30, with difficulty on trail-making (1 error), verbal fluency (10 animals in 60 seconds), and delayed recall (3/5 words, retrieved 1 with cue). Speech slightly hypophonic but articulate. No aphasia.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light. Extraocular movements full, no nystagmus. No vertical gaze palsy. Facial expression mildly hypomimic bilaterally. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline without fasciculations. No jaw jerk.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk mildly reduced in proximal upper extremities bilaterally. Tone increased bilaterally with cogwheel rigidity at wrists and elbows, symmetric. Bradykinesia present bilaterally on finger tapping, hand opening/closing, and pronation/supination, with early decrement — approximately symmetric, left minimally worse than right. Strength 5/5 in all muscle groups. No pronator drift.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact throughout. Proprioception intact at great toes bilaterally. Vibration intact. No cortical sensory loss.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric in biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, and patellar bilaterally. Achilles reflexes 1+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No Babinski sign. No frontal release signs.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally, no dysmetria. Heel-to-shin intact. Rapid alternating movements moderately slowed bilaterally, consistent with bradykinesia rather than cerebellar dysfunction.
neurological_exam.gait: Mildly wide-based gait with reduced arm swing bilaterally. Moderate postural instability on pull test — took 3 retropulsive steps on first attempt, nearly fell on second attempt. Turning requires 5-6 steps (en bloc turning). No freezing of gait. Unable to tandem walk, with lateral veering. Romberg mildly positive with increased sway.
neurological_exam.additional: Orthostatic vital signs: BP supine 142/88 mmHg, HR 72; BP standing at 1 minute 108/64 mmHg, HR 78; BP standing at 3 minutes 98/58 mmHg, HR 80. Patient reported lightheadedness upon standing but did not experience syncope. No tremor observed at rest or with action/posture during a 15-minute observation period. No myoclonus. No dystonia. Mild anterocollis noted. Inspiratory stridor not present.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive gait unsteadiness, slowed movements, and recurrent near-falls over the past 14 months
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Robert Callahan is a 60-year-old right-handed civil engineer who is referred to the neurology outpatient clinic by his primary care physician for evaluation of progressive motor difficulties over the past fourteen months. His wife, who accompanies him, provides much of the history as the patient tends to minimize his symptoms. The onset was insidious, beginning approximately fourteen months ago when his wife noticed he was walking more slowly and seemed 'stiff all over.' He initially attributed this to aging and his sedentary work habits. Over the ensuing months, he developed progressive difficulty with balance, experiencing multiple near-falls — three while walking on uneven surfaces and two while turning quickly. He has not had a complete fall to the ground. His wife reports that his movements have become generally slower; he takes longer to button his shirts, has difficulty cutting food, and his handwriting has become smaller and more cramped. She notes his voice has become softer and his facial expressions less animated, which she initially interpreted as depression. Notably, his wife reports that the motor symptoms appeared to affect both sides approximately equally from the beginning, which she found unusual since her mother, who has essential tremor, had symptoms start on only one side. The patient has never had a resting tremor. His primary care physician prescribed a trial of carbidopa-levodopa 25/100 mg three times daily approximately four months ago. After six weeks at this dose with no clear improvement, the dose was increased to 50/200 mg three times daily for an additional six weeks with only minimal subjective improvement — his wife estimates perhaps ten to fifteen percent better, primarily in his morning stiffness. The constipation predated his motor symptoms by approximately two years, and his erectile dysfunction began around the same time. He reports progressive urinary urgency and has had three urinary tract infections in the past eighteen months, which is unusual for him. Most concerning to the patient is his dizziness upon standing, which has worsened significantly over the past six months. He has had to sit on the edge of the bed for several minutes each morning before standing. On two occasions he felt near-syncopal while standing in line at a store. His wife also notes that he occasionally speaks in a slurred or garbled manner, particularly later in the day when fatigued, and his snoring has become significantly louder over the past year.

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Subtle hyperintense rim along the posterolateral putaminal margins bilaterally
signal_characteristics.T1: Mild volume loss of bilateral putamina
signal_characteristics.SWI: Hypointensity in posterolateral putamina bilaterally, suggesting iron deposition

signal_characteristics.T2: Cruciform hyperintensity in the pons (possible early 'hot cross bun' sign)

signal_characteristics.T1: Mild cerebellar vermian and hemispheric volume loss, beyond expected for age
Age-appropriate cortical volume without focal atrophy
signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Minimal periventricular white matter changes, Fazekas grade 1

volumetrics.putaminal_volume: Reduced bilaterally, below 10th percentile for age
volumetrics.cerebellar_volume: Mildly reduced, approximately 15th percentile for age
volumetrics.midbrain_area: Within normal limits at 1.3 cm² (no hummingbird sign)

additional_observations: No acute infarcts on DWI sequences
No mass lesions or pathological enhancement post-gadolinium
No ventriculomegaly out of proportion to sulcal atrophy
No basal ganglia infarcts or vascular parkinsonism features
Middle cerebellar peduncles appear mildly atrophic
mri.impression: MRI findings suggestive of an atypical parkinsonian syndrome rather than idiopathic Parkinson's disease. The combination of bilateral putaminal volume loss with T2 hyperintense putaminal rim, possible early hot cross bun sign in the pons, cerebellar and pontine atrophy, and middle cerebellar peduncle involvement raises concern for multiple system atrophy, cerebellar type (MSA-C) with parkinsonian features. The midbrain morphology is preserved without hummingbird sign, arguing against progressive supranuclear palsy. No features of normal pressure hydrocephalus.
recommended_actions: DaTscan to assess presynaptic dopaminergic integrity
Autonomic function testing to evaluate for autonomic failure
Clinical correlation with response to levodopa therapy
Consider cardiac MIBG scintigraphy to differentiate PD from MSA

labs.interpretation: Routine laboratory work-up is entirely unremarkable. CBC, BMP, thyroid function, vitamin B12, and ceruloplasmin are all within normal limits. RPR is non-reactive. No reversible or metabolic causes of parkinsonism identified. Inflammatory markers are normal. These results are consistent with a primary neurodegenerative process.

signal_characteristics.pattern: Symmetric bilateral reduction in putaminal and caudate tracer uptake, with greater putaminal involvement

additional_observations: Loss of the normal comma-shaped striatal uptake pattern bilaterally, replaced by a period/dot pattern
Symmetric involvement is atypical for idiopathic Parkinson's disease, which usually shows asymmetric putaminal loss
The degree of bilateral caudate involvement at this clinical stage is more suggestive of an atypical parkinsonian syndrome
output.impression: ABNORMAL DaTscan demonstrating markedly reduced bilateral striatal dopamine transporter uptake, more severely affecting the putamina than the caudates. The pattern confirms presynaptic dopaminergic denervation. However, the symmetric distribution and early caudate involvement is atypical for idiopathic Parkinson's disease and raises concern for an atypical parkinsonian disorder such as multiple system atrophy or progressive supranuclear palsy.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Idiopathic Parkinson's disease with atypically symmetric presentation

recommended_actions: Correlate with clinical features including levodopa responsiveness
Consider cardiac MIBG scintigraphy — preserved uptake would favor MSA over PD

Levodopa_Challenge.clinical_significance: Poor levodopa response (<30% improvement) supports atypical parkinsonism over idiopathic PD
Exacerbation of orthostatic hypotension with levodopa is characteristic of MSA

output.interpretation: Acute levodopa challenge test demonstrates a poor motor response, with only 13.2% improvement in UPDRS-III motor score at 90 minutes. A response of >30% is generally expected in idiopathic Parkinson's disease. The poor levodopa responsiveness, combined with worsening orthostatic hypotension during the challenge, strongly supports an atypical parkinsonian syndrome, particularly multiple system atrophy, rather than idiopathic Parkinson's disease.
abnormal_values_summary: Levodopa response: 13.2% (poor; <30% threshold for good response)

Tilt_Table_Test.clinical_significance: Systolic drop of 48 mmHg and diastolic drop of 30 mmHg; meets criteria for severe neurogenic orthostatic hypotension
Attenuated heart rate response to orthostatic stress, consistent with cardiovascular autonomic failure
Reduced Valsalva ratio indicating cardiovagal impairment

Tilt_Table_Test.value: Absent late phase II recovery and absent phase IV overshoot
Tilt_Table_Test.clinical_significance: Pattern consistent with sympathetic adrenergic failure

QSART.clinical_significance: Markedly reduced postganglionic sudomotor function, length-dependent pattern

Thermoregulatory_Sweat_Test.clinical_significance: Widespread preganglionic and postganglionic sudomotor failure, more pronounced distally; pattern consistent with central autonomic dysfunction as seen in MSA

output.interpretation: Comprehensive autonomic testing reveals severe, multi-domain autonomic failure. Neurogenic orthostatic hypotension is confirmed with a systolic drop of 48 mmHg, attenuated heart rate response, and abnormal Valsalva maneuver demonstrating both cardiovagal and sympathetic adrenergic impairment. Sudomotor testing shows widespread preganglionic and postganglionic dysfunction with 42% anhidrosis on thermoregulatory sweat test. The severity and pattern of autonomic failure, particularly the combination of neurogenic orthostatic hypotension, cardiovagal impairment, and widespread sudomotor failure occurring early in the disease course, is characteristic of multiple system atrophy rather than idiopathic Parkinson's disease, where autonomic dysfunction is typically milder and occurs later.
abnormal_values_summary: Severe neurogenic orthostatic hypotension (systolic drop 48 mmHg)
Attenuated heart rate response to tilt (8 bpm increase)
Absent late phase II and phase IV on Valsalva
Reduced QSART sweat volumes at all sites

Neuropsychological_Battery.clinical_significance: Impaired processing speed, consistent with subcortical pattern
Mildly impaired retrieval-based memory; improves with cueing, suggesting subcortical retrieval deficit rather than cortical encoding failure
Reduced phonemic fluency, consistent with executive-subcortical dysfunction
Impaired set-shifting, consistent with executive dysfunction

Neuropsychological_Battery.test: Wisconsin Card Sorting Test perseverative errors
Neuropsychological_Battery.clinical_significance: Elevated perseverative errors indicating executive dysfunction

output.interpretation: Neuropsychological testing reveals a subcortical-executive pattern of cognitive impairment with reduced processing speed, impaired executive functions (set-shifting, inhibitory control, phonemic fluency, perseveration), and mild retrieval-based memory deficits that improve with cueing. Visuospatial functions, language, and confrontation naming are preserved. This cognitive profile is consistent with frontal-subcortical circuit dysfunction seen in both Parkinson's disease and atypical parkinsonian syndromes including MSA. The severity of executive dysfunction relative to the overall motor disease duration may be slightly greater than expected for typical PD.

Polysomnography.clinical_significance: Markedly elevated REM without atonia, diagnostic of REM sleep behavior disorder

Polysomnography.value: 3 episodes of complex motor behavior during REM
Polysomnography.clinical_significance: Dream enactment behavior confirmed on video-PSG; patient punched and kicked during REM episodes

Polysomnography.value: Nocturnal inspiratory stridor detected during NREM stage 2 and REM
Polysomnography.clinical_significance: Nocturnal stridor is a characteristic and potentially life-threatening feature of MSA due to vocal cord abductor paresis

output.interpretation: Polysomnography reveals confirmed REM sleep behavior disorder with 62% REM without atonia and three observed episodes of complex dream enactment behavior. This is a prodromal marker for alpha-synucleinopathies. Additionally, nocturnal inspiratory stridor was detected, which is a hallmark feature of multiple system atrophy caused by vocal cord abductor paresis and carries prognostic significance as it is associated with increased risk of sudden death. Mild obstructive sleep apnea and elevated periodic limb movements are also noted. The combination of RBD and nocturnal stridor in the setting of parkinsonism with autonomic failure is highly suggestive of MSA.
abnormal_values_summary: REM without atonia: 62% (markedly elevated, diagnostic of RBD)
3 RBD episodes with complex motor behavior

additional_observations: Normal cardiac MIBG uptake with preserved heart-to-mediastinum ratio
output.impression: NORMAL cardiac MIBG scintigraphy with preserved postganglionic sympathetic cardiac innervation. In the context of a patient with parkinsonism and autonomic failure, preserved cardiac MIBG uptake favors multiple system atrophy over idiopathic Parkinson's disease. In PD, cardiac sympathetic denervation typically results in reduced MIBG uptake, whereas in MSA the postganglionic neurons are preserved and the autonomic failure is due to preganglionic/central pathology.
recommended_actions: Correlate with clinical, autonomic, and imaging findings for syndromic diagnosis
Consider genetic testing to exclude rare hereditary causes

results.title: Second consensus statement on the diagnosis of multiple system atrophy
results.authors: Gilman S, Wenning GK, Low PA, et al.
results.summary: Defines probable MSA criteria: sporadic, progressive adult-onset disease with autonomic failure (orthostatic hypotension with >30 mmHg systolic drop or urinary incontinence) plus poorly levodopa-responsive parkinsonism or cerebellar ataxia.

results.title: Movement Disorder Society criteria for the diagnosis of multiple system atrophy
results.authors: Wenning GK, Stankovic I, Krismer F, et al.
results.summary: Updated MDS criteria introducing neuropathologically established, clinically established, and clinically probable MSA. Clinically established MSA requires: (1) sporadic progressive parkinsonism poorly responsive to levodopa OR cerebellar syndrome, PLUS (2) at least one autonomic dysfunction feature, PLUS (3) at least one supportive clinical or MRI feature. Supportive features include stridor, RBD, putaminal or cerebellar atrophy, and hot cross bun sign.

results.title: Prognosis and neuroprotective strategies in MSA
results.summary: Mean survival 6-10 years from symptom onset. Stridor is a red flag for sudden death. No disease-modifying therapy available; management is symptomatic.

output.summary: Current MDS 2022 criteria for clinically established MSA require sporadic progressive poorly levodopa-responsive parkinsonism or cerebellar ataxia, plus autonomic failure, plus at least one supportive feature (stridor, RBD, characteristic MRI findings). This patient appears to meet criteria for clinically established MSA given symmetric poorly levodopa-responsive parkinsonism, severe neurogenic orthostatic hypotension, and supportive MRI features.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Multiple system atrophy, parkinsonian type (MSA-P) with cerebellar features

differential.key_features: Symmetric poorly levodopa-responsive parkinsonism, early severe autonomic failure (neurogenic orthostatic hypotension, urogenital dysfunction), cerebellar and pontine atrophy with hot cross bun sign, putaminal rim sign, nocturnal stridor, preserved cardiac MIBG uptake

Symmetric onset, poor levodopa response, early severe autonomic failure, early postural instability, cerebellar/pontine atrophy, and preserved cardiac MIBG all argue against PD; the initial referral for suspected PD is the diagnostic trap
Early postural instability and falls are consistent, but patient lacks vertical gaze palsy, axial > limb rigidity pattern, and midbrain atrophy (hummingbird sign); cerebellar findings favor MSA
Gait disorder is present, but no ventriculomegaly out of proportion to atrophy, no urinary incontinence (urgency only), and pattern of parkinsonism is not consistent with NPH magnetic gait

optimal_actions.action: Perform detailed neurological examination with emphasis on symmetry of parkinsonism, postural stability, cerebellar signs, and orthostatic vital signs
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Symmetric bilateral rigidity and bradykinesia, early postural instability, no resting tremor, significant orthostatic hypotension with attenuated heart rate response

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with volumetric sequences to evaluate for structural causes and atypical parkinsonism features
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Putaminal atrophy with T2 rim sign, pontine atrophy with hot cross bun sign, cerebellar atrophy — features characteristic of MSA

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive laboratory work-up to exclude reversible causes (CBC, BMP, TSH, B12, ceruloplasmin, RPR)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal results; no reversible or metabolic causes of parkinsonism identified

optimal_actions.action: Request formal levodopa challenge test with supratherapeutic dose to assess dopaminergic responsiveness
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Poor motor response (<30% UPDRS improvement), supporting atypical parkinsonism

optimal_actions.action: Request comprehensive autonomic function testing including tilt table, Valsalva maneuver, QSART, and thermoregulatory sweat test
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Severe multi-domain autonomic failure including neurogenic orthostatic hypotension, cardiovagal impairment, and widespread sudomotor dysfunction

optimal_actions.action: Order DaTscan to confirm presynaptic dopaminergic denervation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Symmetric bilateral reduced striatal uptake with early caudate involvement, atypical pattern for PD

optimal_actions.action: Order polysomnography to evaluate for REM sleep behavior disorder and nocturnal stridor
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Confirmed RBD and nocturnal inspiratory stridor, both supporting MSA diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Consider cardiac MIBG scintigraphy to differentiate PD from MSA
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Preserved cardiac MIBG uptake favoring MSA over PD

optimal_actions.action: Request neuropsychological testing to characterize cognitive profile
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Subcortical-executive dysfunction pattern with impaired processing speed and executive functions

optimal_actions.action: Discuss diagnosis, prognosis, and management plan including midodrine or droxidopa for orthostatic hypotension, ENT evaluation for stridor, and multidisciplinary care referral

critical_actions: Recognize that symmetric parkinsonism with poor levodopa response and early autonomic failure suggests atypical parkinsonism rather than idiopathic PD
Perform formal orthostatic vital signs to quantify neurogenic orthostatic hypotension
Order brain MRI to identify putaminal, pontine, and cerebellar atrophy characteristic of MSA
Assess levodopa responsiveness with adequate trial or formal challenge
Order autonomic function testing to document severity of autonomic failure
Evaluate for nocturnal stridor (potentially life-threatening in MSA) via polysomnography or ENT referral
Do not attribute all symptoms to idiopathic Parkinson's disease despite initial referral framing
contraindicated_actions: Diagnosing idiopathic Parkinson's disease without recognizing red flags for atypical parkinsonism
Escalating levodopa to high doses without recognizing poor response pattern
Prescribing anticholinergic medications (e.g., trihexyphenidyl) which would worsen urinary retention and orthostatic hypotension
Prescribing dopamine agonists (e.g., pramipexole, ropinirole) which carry high risk of orthostatic hypotension in MSA
Ignoring orthostatic hypotension or attributing it solely to tamsulosin/antihypertensive effect
Failing to evaluate for nocturnal stridor, which is associated with sudden death in MSA
Performing deep brain stimulation, which is not effective in MSA
key_reasoning_points: The symmetric onset of parkinsonism without rest tremor is a red flag against idiopathic PD, which typically presents with asymmetric rest tremor
Poor levodopa response (<30% improvement) is a cardinal feature distinguishing atypical parkinsonism from PD, where >30% response is expected
Early and severe autonomic failure (orthostatic hypotension, urogenital dysfunction, constipation) preceding or paralleling motor onset strongly suggests MSA over PD
The combination of cerebellar atrophy, pontine hot cross bun sign, and putaminal rim sign on MRI creates a characteristic MSA imaging signature
Preserved cardiac MIBG uptake differentiates MSA (preganglionic autonomic failure) from PD (postganglionic cardiac denervation)
Nocturnal stridor is pathognomonic for MSA and requires urgent attention as it is associated with sudden death from laryngeal obstruction
REM sleep behavior disorder occurs in both PD and MSA but its combination with severe autonomic failure and stridor points to MSA
The mother's essential tremor is a potential red herring that might lead to consideration of familial tremor disorders
Early postural instability with falls within the first year is a 'red flag' criterion against typical PD per MDS clinical diagnostic criteria for PD

metadata.author: NeuroBench generation system
metadata.clinical_notes: Diagnostic puzzle case featuring multiple system atrophy initially referred as suspected Parkinson's disease. Key challenges include the initial framing bias toward PD, the symmetric presentation without tremor, early severe autonomic failure that could be attributed to medications, and the need to integrate MRI, autonomic testing, levodopa response, and sleep study findings to arrive at the correct MSA diagnosis.
teaching_points: Symmetric parkinsonism without rest tremor should prompt consideration of atypical parkinsonism
Poor levodopa response is a defining feature of MSA and PSP versus idiopathic PD
Early severe autonomic failure (especially neurogenic orthostatic hypotension) in parkinsonism strongly suggests MSA
The hot cross bun sign, putaminal rim sign, and cerebellar/pontine atrophy are characteristic MRI features of MSA
Nocturnal stridor in MSA is life-threatening and requires evaluation
Preserved cardiac MIBG uptake distinguishes MSA from PD
